metadata.summary: Typed AppError with Code, Message, HTTPStatus, Err. HTTP middleware translates to JSON. Wrap at repo boundaries. Use errors.As, never type assertions.

content.title: Go Error Handling
content.summary: Typed AppError with Code, Message, HTTPStatus, Err. HTTP middleware translates to JSON. Wrap at repo boundaries. Use errors.As, never type assertions.

why.p: Go's built-in errors carry no HTTP status, machine-readable code, or structured context. Without a shared error type, each handler invents its own response shape, status mapping, and logging format. A single AppError + middleware pattern enforces consistency and lets errors.Is / errors.As work across wrapping chains.

In production, inconsistent error responses break clients: one handler returns {error: "..."}; another returns {code: "...", message: "..."}. HTTP status codes leak implementation details (500 for a not-found because the developer forgot to map pgx.ErrNoRows). Type assertions or string comparison of errors break when errors are wrapped, causing silent failures. The AppError + Wrap pattern solves these by enforcing a single typed error at every layer, with a middleware that centralizes HTTP mapping.

The Wrap function is the key: it preserves HTTPStatus from inner AppError instead of always demoting to 500. This ensures a not-found error stays a 404 even when wrapped with additional context. Clients rely on these status codes for retry logic: 404 means "don't retry"; 500 means "back off exponentially". Mixing them breaks client behavior. The errors.As check (not type assertion) traverses wrapped errors correctly, catching errors that would be invisible to err.(*AppError) type assertions.

list.item: New Go HTTP service (chi / gin / echo / stdlib net/http) needing consistent JSON error responses
Refactoring a service that returns bare errors.New(...) — wrap with apperror.New* constructors
Adding correlation / trace IDs to error responses for production debugging
Standardizing structured error logging (slog, zerolog, zap)
Library code needing typed errors callable via errors.Is / errors.As
gRPC services — use google.golang.org/grpc/status + codes.Code; HTTPStatus is meaningless
CLI tools — errors go to stderr + exit code; JSON overhead not justified
Public library packages — custom error type forces consumers to depend on your package; prefer sentinel errors
Shallow code paths where fmt.Errorf("%w", err) chains already do the job

rule.statement: Define AppError with Code, Message, HTTPStatus, and Err fields. Implement Error() and Unwrap() so errors.Is/As work across wrapping chains.
rule.rationale: Go's built-in errors carry no HTTP status or machine-readable code. A shared type lets any layer wrap with context while the HTTP boundary uses errors.As to extract the right status.

rule.statement: Always wrap with fmt.Errorf("context: %w", err) — never %v. Use errors.As at HTTP boundaries, never type assertions.
rule.rationale: %v formats but does not wrap; errors.Is/As returns false. Type assertions (*AppError) bypass wrapping and fail silently for nested errors.

rule.statement: Map driver errors (pgx.ErrNoRows, sql.ErrNoRows) to AppError at the repository layer. Never let pgx.* or database/sql errors reach HTTP handlers.
rule.rationale: Driver-specific errors are implementation details. Leaking them couples API responses to the persistence layer choice.

section.name: handler-wrapper-pattern

rule.statement: Use a Handler type (func returning error) + Wrap adapter so handlers can return errors directly instead of writing responses inline. The middleware logs and translates at one place.
rule.rationale: Standard http.HandlerFunc cannot return errors. Without a wrapper, every handler must duplicate status writing and logging logic.

section.reference: Full middleware source with panic recovery

rule.statement: Register panic recovery middleware BEFORE the error-mapping middleware. Panics that reach the error mapper produce blank 500s with no body.
rule.rationale: Middleware executes in registration order for incoming requests. Recovery must wrap the entire handler chain to catch panics from downstream handlers.

rule.statement: Always call w.WriteHeader before json.NewEncoder(w).Encode. Writing the body first causes some frameworks to flush a 200 implicitly before the explicit WriteHeader.
rule.rationale: HTTP status must be set before any bytes are written. Some Go HTTP frameworks buffer, but the stdlib does not.

antipattern.description: Returning package-level mutable *AppError vars directly (e.g., return apperror.ErrNotFound).
antipattern.reason: Any caller can mutate the shared var: ErrNotFound.Message = "X" corrupts the global for all subsequent requests. Always use constructors.

antipattern.description: Using %v instead of %w in fmt.Errorf: fmt.Errorf("op failed: %v", err).
antipattern.reason: %v formats the error string but does not chain the error. errors.Is and errors.As return false through %v-wrapped errors. The errorlint linter catches this.

antipattern.description: Type asserting instead of errors.As: appErr := err.(*apperror.AppError).
antipattern.reason: Type assertion fails for wrapped errors — it only checks the outermost type. errors.As traverses the Unwrap chain and handles wrapped *AppError correctly.

antipattern.description: Wrap always sets HTTPStatus 500, silently promoting 4xx errors to 5xx.
antipattern.reason: Wrapping a NewNotFound (404) with a naive Wrap function produces an AppError with HTTPStatus 500. Wrap must preserve the inner AppError's status when present.

antipattern.description: Leaking driver errors (pgx.ErrNoRows, sql.ErrNoRows) to HTTP handlers.
antipattern.reason: Driver errors couple response shapes to the persistence layer. Map at the repository boundary: pgx.ErrNoRows → apperror.NewNotFound(...).
A write failure leaves the client without a response body. Log the write error even if you cannot re-send the response. errcheck (golangci-lint) flags this.

rule.statement: Run golangci-lint with errorlint, wrapcheck, errcheck, and nilnil enabled. Default golangci-lint configuration misses most error-handling bugs.
rule.rationale: errorlint catches == comparison on errors (use errors.Is). wrapcheck flags unwrapped external errors. errcheck flags ignored return errors.

rule.statement: Never return package-level mutable *AppError vars directly. Use constructor functions that return new instances. Mutable package vars are a footgun: ErrNotFound.Message = "X" corrupts the global for all subsequent requests.
rule.rationale: Each caller should receive its own error instance to modify without affecting others. Constructors guarantee this safety.

rule.statement: At the repository boundary, map database driver errors to AppError. pgx.ErrNoRows becomes apperror.NewNotFound("user"); sql.ErrNoRows becomes apperror.NewNotFound("record"). Never let driver errors propagate to handlers.
rule.rationale: Driver-specific errors are implementation details. Exposing them tightly couples the HTTP API to the database choice. If you migrate from PostgreSQL to MySQL, error types change but the service's error responses should not.

rule.statement: When wrapping an error at a higher layer, preserve the original AppError's HTTPStatus and Code when present. If an inner error is already a 404, wrapping with additional context should keep it 404, not demote it to 500.
rule.rationale: HTTP status communicates the nature of the problem to clients. A not-found error is still not-found even when wrapped; changing it to 500 hides the actual issue and breaks client retry logic (e.g., 404 should not trigger exponential backoff).

rule.statement: Drive error-handling work as scaffold (apperror pkg + middleware) → fill (constructors + wrapping rules) → verify (tests that check status codes and error chains). Require agents to test both success and error paths. For external errors (DB, HTTP), explicitly test wrapping and status mapping.
rule.rationale: Error handling is often skipped in testing. Explicit requirements ensure the agent covers both happy path and failure cases.

rule.statement: Test error chains explicitly: verify that errors.Is and errors.As work correctly across wrapped errors. Test that driver errors (sql.ErrNoRows) are mapped to the right AppError. Test that HTTP status codes match the AppError type.
rule.rationale: Silent failures in error handling go unnoticed until production. Comprehensive error tests catch wrapping and mapping bugs early.

rule.statement: Use structured logging (slog, zap, zerolog) with AppError fields. Log the original error (err) for debugging, not just the message. For 5xx errors, always include stack traces or request context for production debugging.
rule.rationale: Unstructured error logs are hard to parse at scale. Structured logs with AppError fields enable aggregation and alerting. Original errors enable root-cause analysis.

template.purpose: pkg/apperror package: AppError type + constructors + Wrap
Prompt to scaffold apperror package + middleware + tests
